paper_title: HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers route tokens through a handful of experts, and learning-free compression of these layers reduces inference cost without retraining. A subtle obstruction blocks every existing compressor in this family: three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible cycle when merged together, so any score that ranks experts on pairwise signals is structurally blind to which triples are jointly mergeable. We show the obstruction is a precise mathematical object, the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex whose vertices are experts, whose edges carry KL merge barriers, and whose faces carry triplet barriers; Hodge-decomposing the edge-barrier signal isolates the kernel exactly. We turn the diagnostic into a selection objective: HodgeCover greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles, and a hybrid variant of HodgeCover pairs it with off-the-shelf weight pruning on survivors. On three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines on the expert-reduction axis, leads on the aggressive-compression frontier of the hybrid axis, and uniquely balances retained mass across all four Hodge components. These results show that exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure changes which compressor wins at the regime that matters most.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: In trained sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layer, expert may be pairwise compatible with expert B, expert with expert C, and expert with expert A, yet collapsing all three into single expert can still incur an irreducible barrier that no pair of these compatibilities predicts  (Fig. 1)  . Sparselygated Mixture-of-Experts [60] routes each token through only handful of expert sub-networks, and Switch Transformer [13] scaled this construction past the dense compute frontier; the recipe is now Preprint. standard in production-grade open checkpoints such as Mixtral [29], OLMoE [48], Qwen 3.5 [52], and DeepSeek-V3 [39], each of which hosts dozens to hundreds of feed-forward experts per MoE layer while routing only small constant subset to any one token. Compressing such layer without retraining is therefore first-order concern: the parameter mass lives in experts that are inactive at any inference step, and learning-free compressor would let practitioners reuse pretrained MoE checkpoint on smaller fleets without fine-tuning, knowledge distillation, or re-pretraining. The dominant family of such compressors ranks experts on pairwise signals: REAP [31] uses output saliency, REAM [28] reweights routing through saliency-aware merge scores, MC-SMoE [35] clusters experts via routing-cosine similarity, and STUN [32] structurally prunes experts before Wanda [62] unstructured sparsity. Pairwise rankings, however, are blind to structural property of mergeability: three experts can be pairwise mergeable yet collectively non-mergeable when their merge-barriers form an irreducible cycle. Methods that do consume triplet inputs, such as hypergraph spectral cuts [72] and triplet-loss penalties [58], aggregate triplets into sum-of-pairs surrogate or binary veto rather than isolating the topological residue. The harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian [36, 57] is the unique linear-algebraic object that captures this residue, and prior topological deep learning [6] has used it as diagnostic tool but never as parameter-selection objective. 1 1 + 2 To this end, we introduce HodgeCover, learning-free expert-selection objective that turns the harmonic kernel into coverage criterion. From sparse MoE layer with experts we build 2-dimensional simplicial complex whose vertices are experts, whose edges carry pairwise KL merge-barriers, and whose 2-faces carry triplet barriers, and we apply the simplicial Laplacian [36] L1 = 2 to Hodge-decompose the edge-barrier signal into gradient, curl, and harmonic components. HodgeCover then picks survivor experts by greedy submodular coverage [51] of the top-p% harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles, weighted by saliency score adapted from REAP [31]; non-survivors are pruned with router redirect to the closest survivor. The hybrid HodgeCover+Wanda pairs this expert-axis compressor with unstructured Wanda [62] pruning on survivor weights, composing two orthogonal compression axes inside single learning-free pipeline. We summarize our contributions as follows. We define the simplicial mergeability complex of sparse MoE layer and identify its higher-order mergeability obstruction with the harmonic component of its Hodge decomposition, verifying this component is non-trivial (29 62% of per-layer barrier energy) across three MoE families (3). We instantiate HodgeCover, learning-free objective that greedily covers the top-p% harmoniccritical edges and triplet-critical triangles, and hybrid HodgeCover+Wanda that pairs it with unstructured Wanda pruning on survivors (4, Fig. 3). At 66% expert reduction across three MoE scales, HodgeCover+Wanda achieves the best perplexity on every model and outperforms STUN+Wanda [32] by up to +12.6 pp downstream accuracy, while matched ablation that skips the Hodge step costs 5.74 pp on Qwen 3.5-35B (5, 5.4).

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: MoE expert pruning and merging is static post-training family that ranks experts on pairwise signals scored from small calibration set: REAP [31] on output saliency, REAM [28] on saliencyaware merge scoring, MC-SMoE [35] and HC-SMoE [7] on routing-cosine clustering, and STUN [32] on structured-then-unstructured decision composed with Wanda [62] and OWL [68]. Calibrationfree pretrained-weight scores [41, 44] and router-gated weight metrics [66] relax the supervision of the same pairwise rule, while evolutionary search over expert subsets [40, 42], prune-then-recompose redistribution [67], and unified slim-trim trimming [24] relax the one-shot constraint; learning-based independent-expert training and merging [34, 71] and dense-to-sparse upcycling [30] sit orthogonally on the capacity-construction axis. Every method aggregates the merge landscape into edge-level pairwise scores, and none represents the higher-order obstruction that arises when three experts are pairwise mergeable but not jointly mergeable. HodgeCover is the first learning-free expert-selection objective that operates on the harmonic kernel of the layers simplicial mergeability complex. Sparsity-based weight pruning compress LLMs at the weight-matrix level along three axes. Oneshot unstructured pruning ranks weights from calibration activations (Wanda [62], SparseGPT [15]) and extends magnitudeand movement-pruning lineage [22, 56]; structured pruning removes coupled channels, heads, or transformer blocks (LLM-Pruner [45], ShortGPT [46]); post-training quantization compresses bit-width rather than count (GPTQ [16], AWQ [37], SmoothQuant [65], 2 OmniQuant [59]). All these axes operate on weight matrices and cannot exploit the structural redundancy among MoE experts; on sparse MoE checkpoint they leave expert-count compression entirely on the table. HodgeCover+Wanda composes the two axes inside one learning-free pipeline: Stage-1 expert reduction on the topological signal, Stage-2 unstructured Wanda on survivor weights. Topological and spectral methods on neural networks apply algebraic-topology and spectral machinery along two complementary lines. The architectural line installs Hodge and simplicial operators as message-passing primitives or feature layers (geometric deep learning [6], simplicial neural networks [5, 11], topological GNNs [27]), with foundational Hodge theory traced to Eckmann [12] and modernized for discrete data [36, 57, 70]. The diagnostic line summarises Betti numbers, persistence diagrams, and Hodge spectra of trained networks for generalization analysis [20, 50, 54, 70]; the closest matrix-level precedents that act on spectral signal, spectral sparsification [61] and spectral pruning [63], operate at the dyadic level and never lift to higher-order simplicial structure. Across both lines topology is consumed as input or summarised as output, and almost no prior work operationalizes it into parameter-selection objective. We lift the Hodge decomposition from analysis tool to engineering selection criterion that drives practical compressor. Tripletand hypergraph-aware methods in compression and clustering encode higher-order relational structure as hyperedges or triplet constraints in two distinct families. Hypergraph spectral methods reduce triplet (or larger) cliques to clique-expansion Laplacian for cuts [1, 72], higherorder Cheeger inequalities [33, 43], motif-based partitioning [3], and hypergraph convolution and attention [2, 14]. Metric learning encodes triplets as scalar margin constraints in pair-contrastive form [21] or triplet form (FaceNet [58], deep ranking [64]). All these methods aggregate higher-order inputs into sum-of-pairs surrogate or binary veto, never separating the gradient, curl, and harmonic components of the signal. We Hodge-decompose triplet-augmented edge barriers and show (5.4) that the harmonic component is the load-bearing piece: soft-triplet ablation that skips the decomposition costs 5.74 pp downstream-task average accuracy on Qwen 3.5-35B at 66% compression.

Let = be the dropped experts. We use the REAP redirect surgery [31]: expert weights of each are erased and its router logit is folded into single survivor π(i) S, so the gating distribution renormalizes onto at no extra forward pass. Where REAP picks π(i) to be the cosine-nearest survivor of expert in router-key space, we pick it to be the nearest survivor under Hodge-weighted barrier that gives extra weight to harmonic edges: π(i) = arg min jS (cid:16) bij 1 + α bharm,{i,j} (cid:17) , , (13) where the strength α > 0 controls how much edges that carry their own harmonic mass are penalized: routing non-survivor mass through such an edge would re-introduce the very obstruction that the survivor set was selected to cover. We adopt the convention that the harmonic factor is 1 when = 0 (equivalently, when bharm = 0). These three steps run independently per MoE layer; the cross-layer budget allocator that turns global compression rate into the per-layer counts {kℓ}ℓ is given in App. B. Algorithm pseudocode, hyperparameter defaults, the cross-layer allocator, and baseline-axis comparison grid  (Table 3)  versus other learning-free MoE compressors benchmarked in 5 are in App. and App. C. 4.2 HodgeCover+Wanda HodgeCover targets the expert-count axis; we obtain hybrid compressor, HodgeCover+Wanda, by composing it with an off-the-shelf one-shot weight pruner. Stage 1 runs HodgeCover at fixed expert-count drop rate r1, yielding the survivor set and the redirected router. Stage 2 applies unstructured Wanda [62] on the survivor weights at the residual sparsity needed to reach the total target rate, reusing the same calibration corpus at no additional forward-pass. Stage 1 is the contribution; Stage 2 is plug-and-play (any calibration-aware unstructured weight pruner could replace Wanda). The pseudocode, the residual-sparsity protocol that selects r2 given Stage-1 rate r1, and the per-cell numerical values of r1 and r2 used are in App. B.1 and B.4.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Setup We benchmark three open-weight sparse MoE backbones spanning two expert-count regimes and two scales: OLMoE-1B-7B [48] (16 MoE layers, 64 experts), Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B and Qwen 3.5-122BA10B [52] (40 / 48 MoE layers, 256 experts). Each backbone is compressed at 33% and 66% of expert count. Each baseline follows its source papers released implementation up to the calibration-footing reductions detailed in App. B.7: REAP [31], REAM [28], MC-SMoE [35] (w/o KD reduction), and STUN [32]. HodgeCover (ours) uses the same uniform per-layer convention as REAP and REAM (App. E.1). Calibration uses 2,048 C4-train tokens [53]; evaluation reports WikiText-103 perplexity [47], C4 perplexity, and nine downstream tasks via the LM Evaluation Harness [18]: ARC-c and ARC-e [9], BoolQ [8], HellaSwag [69], MMLU 5-shot [25], PIQA [4], TruthfulQA-MC2 [38], WinoGrande [55], and GSM8K 8-shot [10]. We summarize the nine downstream-task accuracies by their unweighted arithmetic mean, the downstream-task average (DS-Avg). Beyond the four published baselines we also report four ablations of HodgeCover that share its calibration corpus and per-layer expert budget but replace the topological objective with no-triangle, soft-tripletpenalty, hard-triplet-veto, or topology-blind alternative [72]; precise constructions, hardware, harness configuration, and per-method allocator descriptions are in App. E.1 and App. D. 5.2 Main results HodgeCover+Wanda has the best C4 perplexity on every (model, rate) cell in Table 1 and the best WikiText perplexity on every 66% cell, with WikiText margins of 1.43.8 points over STUN+Wanda at 66% on the three backbones; at 33%, STUN+Wanda is ahead on WikiText for the two smaller backbones (OLMoE and Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B) at the cost of 1.72.2 C4 perplexity points. On the four Qwen (model, rate) cells the downstream margin is large and structural: at 66% on Qwen 3.535B, HodgeCover+Wanda gains +12.6 pp DS-Avg over STUN+Wanda (74.6% vs. 62.0%), driven primarily by GSM8K (85.5% vs. 22.1%) with smaller 8.9 pp lead on MMLU (78.0% vs. 69.2%); the same pattern holds at 66% on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B. sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: Pairwise expert-merge scores are structurally blind to higher-order property of MoE mergeability: three experts can be pairwise compatible yet collectively form an irreducible cycle. The simplicial mergeability complex makes this cycle visible, and its Hodge decomposition isolates it as harmonic component that no aggregation of pairwise scores can express. Covering the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles with learning-free objective drives the best aggressive-compression frontier across three production MoE scales, evidence that the harmonic kernel of learned structure can carry content that pairwise scoring cannot reach. Limitations and Broader Impact. HodgeCover stays inside the learning-free family: recovering the last few perplexity points typically requires fine-tuning or knowledge-distillation step that is orthogonal to our pipeline. Plan-time on 35B-parameter MoE is dominated by barrier computation; this is one-shot offline cost amortized across compression rates because the simplicial complex is cached. Our evaluation is restricted to language MoE; multimodal and reinforcement-learning post-trained checkpoints are deferred, although the construction is modality-agnostic. As with other compression methods, lowering the inference cost of large pretrained MoE models without retraining both democratizes access and accelerates deployment of insufficiently aligned checkpoints; the practitioner remains responsible for the audit posture of any pretrained MoE checkpoint they deploy.

sections.title: A Hodge Decomposition Primer and Implementation Details
sections.content: This appendix carries the full background and proofs deferred from Section 3. App. A.1 reviews the abstract simplicial complex; App. A.2 defines the boundary operators and proves the chain identity; App. A.3 constructs the combinatorial 1-Hodge Laplacian, characterizes its kernel, and reports the Euler-Poincaré count for β1(K); App. A.4 states and proves the discrete Hodge decomposition; App. A.5 derives explicit projection formulas via the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse; App. A.8 documents the effect of edge thresholding on the spectrum and motivates working with the complete edge set; App. A.9 analyzes the sensitivity of bharm to the curated triangle set ; App. A.10 defines the frequency-weighted merge operation and its all-zero-frequency guard; App. A.11 fixes the calibration corpus; App. A.12 reports the per-layer ranges, gradient/curl companion curves, and the representative discordance margin used in Figure 2. We follow the textbook treatments of Hatcher [23], Munkres [49] for classical homology, and Eckmann [12], Friedman [17], Goldberg [19], Horak and Jost [26], Lim [36], Schaub et al. [57], Zheng et al. [70] for the discrete Hodge framework. A.1 Abstract simplicial complexes An abstract simplicial complex on finite vertex set = {1, . . . , n} is family 2V of nonempty subsets that is closed under taking non-empty subsets: σ and = τ σ implies τ K. Elements σ with σ = + 1 are q-simplices; the set of q-simplices is denoted Fq(K). We work exclusively with {0, 1, 2}: vertices, edges, triangles. Throughout this paper = (V, E, ) with = F0(K) = {1, . . . , n}, = F1(K) the complete pairwise edge set (cid:0)V (cid:1) (cid:1), and = F2(K) (cid:0)V curated triangle set whose construction is given in App. A.9. 2 To do linear algebra on we orient every simplex. Fix any total ordering < on . For each simplex {v0, . . . , vq} Fq(K) with v0 < v1 < < vq, we choose the canonical lexicographically-ordered representative [v0, . . . , vq] as its orientation; an odd permutation of these indices represents the opposite orientation. Equivalently, an oriented q-simplex is the equivalence class of an ordered (q + 1)-tuple of distinct vertices modulo even permutations. The space of q-chains is the real vector space Cq(K) = RFq(K), with the standard basis indexed by oriented q-simplices. For = 0, 1, 2 the dimensions are dim C0 = = n, dim C1 = E, and dim C2 = . We endow each Cq(K) with the Euclidean inner product in the lexicographic basis. This is the unweighted choice; weighted variants are discussed in Remark 3. A.2 Boundary operators and the chain identity The boundary of an oriented simplex is the formal alternating sum of its oriented faces. Concretely, 1 : C1(K) C0(K), 2 : C2(K) C1(K), 1 [i, j] = [j] [i], 2 [i, j, k] = [j, k] [i, k] + [i, j], < j, < < k. Extending linearly, 1 is represented in the canonical bases by the signed vertex-edge incidence matrix B1 {1, 0, +1}V of the underlying graph; 2 is represented by the signed edge-triangle incidence matrix B2 {1, 0, +1}ET . Inexpressibility example (Remark 1). Define an individualistic vertex selector as any algorithm that assigns each expert scalar score h(i) depending only on quantities indexed at the i-th vertex (per-expert saliencies, per-vertex sums of pairwise barriers, per-vertex sorted-pairwise-barrier (cid:80) statistics, etc.) and returns ˆS = arg maxS=k iS h(i). REAP, REAM centroid selection, MCSMoE, and the published STUN family all fit this template. HodgeCover does not, because Φ depends on the critical-edge incidence sets {NE(i)}i, which are joint properties of that need not factor into sum over vertices. Construction. Take = {1, 2, 3, 4}, = 2, and consider the three problem instances {a, b, c} where the critical-edge set E,(r) is the corresponding perfect matching of K4: (cid:80) E,(a) = (cid:8){1, 2}, {3, 4}(cid:9), E,(b) = (cid:8){1, 3}, {2, 4}(cid:9), E,(c) = (cid:8){1, 4}, {2, 3}(cid:9). Any individualistic selector ˆS = arg maxS=k iS h(i) therefore returns the same pair ˆS on all three instances (the score vector is the same constant on every vertex, with tie-breaking inherent to the selector). For any fixed pair ˆS = {i, j} and any matching Mr, the coverage CE( ˆS)/E,(r) equals 1 if ˆS meets both edges of Mr and 1/2 if ˆS meets only one. The key observation is that ˆS = {i, j} meets only one edge of Mr exactly when {i, j} is one of the two matching edges of Mr (in that case, {i, j} shares both endpoints with one matching edge and is disjoint from the other). Among the three matchings of K4, every fixed pair {i, j} is itself one of the matching edges in exactly one matching, so the worst-case coverage of ˆS over the three instances is exactly 1/2. HodgeCover, by contrast, sees E,(r) explicitly per instance and selects pair ˆS that meets both edges of Mr, achieving coverage 1 in every instance. This matches the published default of REAP [31] and REAM [28], and we adopt it as the HodgeCover convention. MC-SMoE [35] and STUN+Wanda [32, 62] use their own native global allocators in our experiments (a global frequency-threshold rule for MC-SMoE and global agglomerative-similarity rule for STUN+Wanda), faithful to their original papers. Compressibility-weighted allocator (alternative; not used for HodgeCover main-table results). The uniform allocator ignores per-layer topology. natural alternative weights the drop budget by per-layer compressibility score σ(ℓ) = max(cid:0)1 ρharm(ℓ), ε0 (cid:1) (layers with less harmonic mass are easier to compress), allocating dℓ = (cid:4)R (cid:80) σ(ℓ) ℓ σ(ℓ) (cid:5), followed by the same one-expert rounding-remainder pass and the dℓ nℓ 1 floor as Eq. 32. [28], Lasby et al. [31] use; using different allocator on the HodgeCover row would conflate the survivor-selection objective with the cross-layer budget assignment. All HodgeCover and REAP / REAM rows in Section 5 therefore use Eq. 32. [62] unmodified, applied independently to each surviving expert and to the shared linear layers of the MoE block (gate, up, down projections in the SwiGLU MLP, depending on architecture). For completeness, Algorithm 2 states the per-matrix loop. The single hyperparameter is the Stage-2 weight sparsity r2, chosen so that the combined expert-axis and weight-axis compression matches the target total compression rate of the comparison cell; we never retune the Wanda saliency or the row/column choice. The dominant cost of HodgeCover at single MoE layer is the pairwise-barrier sweep: for experts, computing bij for every pair requires (cid:0)n (cid:1) short forward passes on D. The tripletbarrier sweep adds 500 further forward passes, by construction independent of n. The Hodge (cid:1) edges and 500 projection on Kτ is sparse linear-algebra operation on graph with = (cid:0)n triangles; for our scales (n 256) it costs at most few seconds per layer (App. A.5). The greedy survivor loop on lines 1016 of Algorithm 1 is O(k (E + )) in marginal-gain evaluations and fits in well under one second. Per model. Summed across all MoE layers, plan-time scales as n2 in barrier sweeps. On the hardware used in this paper, the dominant offline cost is the pairwise-barrier sweep itself (one short forward pass on per pair), with the Hodge projection contributing under two seconds per layer at = 256 and at most = 500. We measure the entire learning-free HodgeCover pipeline (calibration corpus loading, barrier matrix plus triplet-barrier computation at the Betti-maximizing τ , Hodge projection, survivor selection, and router-redirect surgery) on Qwen3.5-35B at approximately 480 seconds end-to-end, against measurement of approximately 25 seconds for the published REAP pipeline run on the same calibration corpus and the same hardware: roughly 19 overhead, paid once and offline, amortized across the entire downstream evaluation. The full per-method, per-model wall-clock breakdown, including the corresponding measurements on OLMoE-1B-7B and Qwen3.5-122B-A10B, is reported in App. E.5 alongside the secondary metrics. B.6 Baseline-axis comparison grid Table 3 summarizes how HodgeCover differs from the seven other learning-free MoE compressors benchmarked in this paper, along three axes: which scoring signal each method assigns to experts (or expert pairs); whether surviving experts have their weights perturbed by the compression step; and plan-time complexity per single MoE layer with experts, expert hidden dimension d, calibration corpus D, and curated triangle set . All table entries are learning-free as we report them; the only entry whose published form requires learning step is MC-SMoE [35], addressed in the table footnote and detailed in App. B.7. HodgeCover is the only entry that uses the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian as its scoring signal while keeping survivor weights bit-exact. The field values for each external baseline have been verified against its original published description. Two patterns are worth flagging. First, every external baseline that perturbs survivor weights does so via learning-free heuristic that treats the merge as per-pair operation [28, 32, 35]; the higher-order representational risk of those merges (e.g., merging an expert that carries irreducible harmonic mass) is exactly what the harmonic-kernel coverage objective in HodgeCover was designed to sidestep, by keeping survivors bit-exact. Second, the plan-time gap between methods that score experts from single calibration sweep (MC-SMoE, STUN+Wanda, and saliency-only reading of REAP leading calibration term d2) and methods that assemble pairwise-KL barrier matrix from O(n2) per-pair operations (HodgeCover, Greedy-Barrier, Triplet-Penalty, Triplet-Hypergraph leading term n2 d2) is factor of up to n2. The official REAP and REAM reference implementations additionally run every expert on every calibration token, raising their leading term to d2 and narrowing the asymptotic gap to factor of n. Either gap is substantial at = 256, but the entire HodgeCover pipeline runs offline, once, before any downstream evaluation, so the offline plan-time cost is amortized across all inference calls thereafter. 24 Table 3: Baseline-axis comparison grid. Survivor weights is whether the method modifies surviving experts weights at compression time; Learning is whether the method requires any gradient-based fine-tuning, knowledge distillation, or LoRA pass beyond the calibration forward passes. Plan-time complexity is per single MoE layer; is the expert count, the expert hidden dimension, the calibration token count, and the curated triangle-set size. Our ablation marks baselines that we built and report only in our papers evaluation set; they are not external published methods. At our 2,048-token calibration scale this KD pass overfits and regresses quality on every model in our evaluation set. We therefore report MC-SMoE without the KD step, matching the most common reduction adopted by subsequent MoE-compression literature (App. B.7). The plan-time row for REAP [31] charges the cost of the observer in the official implementation, which runs every expert on every calibration token to populate the unified pruning + merging metrics. saliency-only implementation admits lower O(D d2) bound; we report the observer-cost form here for parity with the other baselines, all of which are charged for the dominant cost of their official reference implementations. B.7 Baseline implementation notes This subsection records implementation choices for the external baselines in Table 3 that depart from default reading of the original paper. Three are load-bearing for the headline numbers in Section 5: MC-SMoE without KD. Li et al. [35] (Section 4.1, Table 5) report MC-SMoE with mandatory 20,000-step post-merge knowledge-distillation pass against the original SMoE teacher; Table 5 of that work shows substantial degradation when the KD pass is removed in the original papers training-time data regime. In our single-pass learning-free regime the calibration corpus is 2,048 C4 tokens (App. A.11); we implemented the KD module and ran it at this scale, found that it overfits the calibration corpus and degrades held-out perplexity on WikiText-103 and C4 validation by margin larger than the gain it provides on the calibration set itself, and accordingly removed it. This matches the most common MC-SMoE reduction reported by subsequent MoE-compression literature [28, 31, 32], which also benchmark MC-SMoE without the KD pass for comparability with learning-free baselines. The headline implication is that our MC-SMoE numbers are learning-free re-implementation, are not directly comparable to the published MC-SMoE numbers in the regime that paper targets, and are reported to keep all benchmarked methods on the same calibration footing rather than as faithful reproduction. REAP, REAM, and STUN+Wanda. We use the published reference implementations of REAP [31], REAM [28], and STUN [32] unmodified, except that all three are run on the same fixed 2,048-token C4 calibration corpus (App. A.11) to ensure all methods are evaluated under identical calibration conditions, rather than the 128to 1024-sequence regimes used in their respective evaluation tables. All other implementation details (cross-layer expert-budget allocator, Stage-2 Wanda saliency, etc.) follow each baselines original implementation. Greedy-Barrier, Triplet-Penalty soft, and Triplet-Hypergraph hard are not external baselines but ablations that we built specifically to isolate components of HodgeCover; none of the three appears in published MoE-compression literature. All three replace HodgeCovers coverage-based survivor selection with greedy union-find merge sort, and aggregate the resulting merge groups via the same frequency-weighted average as REAM and MC-SMoE (App. A.10); they differ only in how triplet-barrier information enters the merge sort. Greedy-Barrier sorts all pairs {i, j} in ascending order of pairwise barrier bij and greedily unions them via union-find loop that terminates when exactly components remain. No Hodge decomposition, no harmonic weighting, and no triplet input. It isolates the contribution of the pairwise-barrier signal alone. Triplet-Penalty soft reuses 25 Greedy-Barriers union-find loop with the edge cost bij replaced by bij (1 + αT pij), where pij is the layer-normalized mean of triplet barriers bijk over all (i, j, k) that contain edge {i, j} and αT 0 is the penalty strength. The same triplet table used by HodgeCover is consumed, but no Hodge decomposition is performed; it isolates the contribution of the harmonic kernel beyond plain sum-of-triplet-barriers softening. Triplet-Hypergraph hard reuses Greedy-Barriers union-find loop with binary triplet veto inspired by hypergraph-cut compression [72]: candidate union is accepted only if every triple of experts inside the resulting component (when of size 3) has bijk τT , where τT is the layers 50th-percentile triplet barrier; any union that would create component carrying high-barrier triple is rejected and the loop continues with the next-best edge. It isolates the cost of binary triangle constraint vs. the soft penalty of Triplet-Penalty. All three ablations therefore use the same frequency-weighted merge aggregation as REAM/MC-SMoE; the bit-exact-survivor row of Table 3 for HodgeCover is what isolates the zero-and-redirect surgery from the topological-coverage objective. The three ablations are revisited alongside the Section 5.4 ablation table. Hyperparameter and Design-Choice Sensitivity This appendix records the rationale behind, and the analytical robustness of, each scalar hyperparameter in HodgeCover. App. C.1 lists the defaults and how they were set; App. C.2 discusses the critical-simplex fractions p, qT ; App. C.3 discusses the coverage weights λe, λt; App. C.4 discusses the harmonic-redirect strength α; App. C.5 comments on the sensitivity of HodgeCover to the construction of the triangle set ; and App. C.6 comments on the sensitivity to the choice of merge metric (KL versus alternatives) used to populate the barriers bij and bijk. The hyperparameter values reported here are the values used for every model and every reported compression rate in this paper; no per-model tuning is performed and no values are swept post-submission. C.1 Defaults and how they were set The frozen defaults are = qT = 20%, λe = 1.0, λt = 0.5, α = 3.0, 500. Each value was set on single, MMLU-stratified Qwen3.5-35B layer (layer 19, the median-depth MoE layer) by inspecting the HodgeCover output qualitatively on separate 2,048-token C4-train sample disjoint from the calibration corpus (App. A.11): whether contained the edges that the per-layer harmonic-magnitude histogram identified as heavy; whether the survivor set spread roughly uniformly across the harmonic-incidence-sorted ranking of experts; and whether the redirect map π avoided routing non-survivors through edges with above-mean harmonic magnitude. Layer 19 is compressed normally and appears in all reported main-table evaluations on Qwen3.5-35B; the held-out label refers only to the disjoint C4-train sample used for hyperparameter inspection. C.2 Critical-simplex fractions p, qT The fractions and qT control the size of the harmonic-critical edge set and the triplet-critical triangle set that the coverage objective in Eq. 10 must cover. They have clean problem-side interpretation: is the part of where the irreducible higher-order obstruction concentrates, and is the part where joint pairwise merging amplifies pairwise cost. Two analytical properties bound how much HodgeCover can move under perturbations of and qT . Saturation at large p, qT . As p, qT 100%, every edge and every sampled triangle becomes critical, so = and = and the coverage gains in Eq. 11 reduce to per-expert vertex degrees in K. Since has the complete pairwise edge set, every expert has the same vertex degree in E, so the edge-coverage gain collapses to constant; the triangle gain remains informative through the (non-uniform) triangle incidence in . The objective therefore degrades smoothly into saliencyplus-triangle-degree selector at the saturation boundary. Vanishing at small p, qT . As p, qT 0%, the critical sets shrink to single edges or single triangles, and the coverage objective becomes saliency-plus-noise. HodgeCover is therefore sensitive to p, qT being too small. The default = qT = 20% sits well inside the non-saturated, nonvanishing regime for every model (cid:1) and {64, 256}, [ 400, 6500 ], far from either boundary. We in this paper: at = (cid:0)n did not run full sweep of p, qT across the three model scales pre-submission, and accordingly we do not report empirical sensitivity bands; the analytical robustness above is the substantive claim about p, qT . 2 26 C.3 Coverage weights λe, λt The coverage weights λe and λt trade off saliency against the two coverage gains in Eq. 11. Because both coverage gains are normalized by the size of the corresponding critical set, every marginal-gain term lies in [0, 1] for every layer of every model; the weights λe, λt have uniform interpretation across models. Setting λe = λt = 0 recovers pure saliency-ranked selector on the REAP saliency score [31]; setting λe 1, λt 1 makes saliency tiebreaker only. The default λe = 1.0, λt = 0.5 gives the edge-coverage gain parity with saliency and the triangle-coverage gain half that weight, reflecting that triplet barriers are noisier than pairwise barriers (the triplet sweep produces 500 samples versus (cid:0)n (cid:1) pairwise samples). formal sensitivity sweep over these weights at multiple 2 model scales is left for future work. C.4 Harmonic-redirect strength α The redirect cost in Eq. 13 interpolates between pure pairwise-barrier nearest-survivor redirect (α = 0) and strongly harmonic-penalized redirect (α 1). The default α = 3.0 is large enough to dominate the small differences in bij that occur for nearby survivors but small enough not to override large pairwise-barrier gaps: at the default, two survivors with equal bij to non-survivor break the tie according to the harmonic component, and α = 3 corresponds to 4 penalty on the most-harmonic edge in the layer relative to zero-harmonic edge (i.e., 1 + 3 1 = 4 versus 1). C.5 Triangle-set construction The sensitivity of bharm to the choice of triangle set is analyzed in App. A.9. There we contrast three regimes: = (pairwise-only; the curl subspace im(2) vanishes and the harmonic component reduces to the graph cycle space ker(1), which then carries the entire non-gradient signal), = (cid:0)V (cid:1) (every triple face; combinatorially intractable at = 256 where (cid:0)V (cid:1) > 2.7 106), and the curated 500 used here. The third regime is what all results in this paper use; the cap of 500 was chosen at the high end of what the triplet-barrier sweep (one forward pass per triple) can finish in the same wall-clock budget as the pairwise sweep. We did not run paired ablation across multiple values of pre-submission. 3 3 C.6 Merge metric All barriers bij, bijk in this paper are computed from the KL divergence between the original layers output distribution and the merged layers output distribution on the calibration corpus (App. A.10). KL is the natural choice in the language-modeling regime: the per-token output is categorical distribution over the vocabulary, and the calibration objective in Eq. 1 is itself KL. Other f-divergences (JensenShannon, Hellinger), or distributional metrics on activations rather than outputs (cosine on activated-output vectors, Wasserstein-1 on activation distributions), would all populate bij, bijk with positive scalars and could in principle be substituted; we did not run paired comparison pre-submission. The chain-complex structure of and the Hodge decomposition itself depend only on which pairs are in and which triples are in , not on the metric used to populate the edge-supported signal b, so metric swap is local to the barrier sweep.

sections.title: D Experimental setup details
sections.content: This appendix supports Section 5.1. D.1 Models and checkpoints We benchmark three open-weight sparse MoE backbones, all pulled from their canonical releases without fine-tuning. OLMoE-1B-7B (allenai/OLMoE-1B-7B-0924, 16 MoE layers, 64 experts per layer, top-2 routing) instantiates the small-model regime. Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B (40 MoE layers, 256 experts per layer, top-8 routing) and Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B (48 MoE layers, 256 experts per layer, top-8 routing) instantiate the high-cardinality regime; we use the base checkpoints with reinforcement-learning post-training disabled to keep the calibration distribution and the baseline router both well-defined. Tokenizer, vocabulary, and positional encoding follow each backbones default configuration. 27 D.2 Compression rates and the per-layer allocator ℓ nℓ experts in total and 66% removes 0.66 (cid:80) Compression rates are quoted as fractions of total expert count across the model: 33% removes 0.33 (cid:80) ℓ nℓ. The per-layer survivor counts {kℓ} that realise this total are produced by each baselines source-paper allocator (App. E.1). We chose 33% and 66% rather than 25%/50%/75% because the two rates straddle the "easy / hard" frontier reported by the prior MoE-compression literature (REAP, REAM, and STUN+W all report 50%-rate numbers in the modest-loss regime; 66% is past the regime in which the published hybrids retain their downstream profile). D.3 Calibration and evaluation Calibration uses 2,048 tokens drawn from the C4 train split [53] at random seed 42, fixed across all methods. Tokens are concatenated to the models training context length and truncated to fit the calibration budget; we did not subsample by document, since the relevant signal for the per-layer barriers is distributional rather than per-document. Evaluation reports WikiText-103 perplexity [47], C4 perplexity (held-out validation split, never overlapping with calibration), and nine downstream tasks via the LM Evaluation Harness [18] at version v0.4.x: ARC-Challenge and ARC-Easy [9] (zero-shot, accuracy-norm), BoolQ [8] (zero-shot), HellaSwag [69] (zero-shot, accuracy-norm), MMLU [25] (5-shot, macro across the 57 subjects), PIQA [4] (zero-shot), TruthfulQA-MC2 [38] (zero-shot), WinoGrande [55] (zero-shot), and GSM8K [10] (8-shot, exact-match strict). DS-Avg in this paper is the unweighted macro of the nine downstream accuracies. D.4 Hardware and reproducibility Calibration, plan-time, and topology audits run on single workstation with 2 NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell GPUs (98 GB each, 252 GB RAM, 64 CPU cores) for the primary runs; the OLMoE Round-N benchmarks were run on paired A6000 workstation, and the Qwen 3.5 ablations and topology audits were distributed across H200 nodes for parallel throughput. Inference throughput is measured with PyTorch 2.x and Hugging Face transformers at the models default context length, batch size 1, 128-token decode, repeated 5 times with the median reported.

sections.title: E Additional Results
sections.content: This appendix supports Section 5. It is organized as five subsections: description of the per-method cross-layer allocator and the four HodgeCover ablations (App. E.1), the full hybrid axis with matchedcontrol hybrids and per-rate Pareto plot (App. E.2), the per-layer retained mass trajectories for the harmonic / gradient / curl / triplet-barrier components on the two backbones not shown in the main body (App. E.3), the per-task ablation breakdown across all three backbones (App. E.4), and the plan-time, throughput, routing entropy, and dead-expert ratio (App. E.5). E.1 Per-method allocator and ablation construction Per-method baseline implementation. For each baseline we follow the source papers released implementation; the brief notes below summarize the survivor-selection rule used in our experiments. REAP [31] ranks experts within each MoE layer by an output-saliency score and drops the lowestranking ones, applying the uniform per-layer cut of Eq. 32. REAM [28] reduces every MoE layer to fixed target survivor count by saliency-guided clustering of experts and merging non-centroid members into their nearest centroid. MC-SMoE [35] uses normalized routing frequencies to mark global pool of low-frequency experts as non-dominant subject to per-layer "at-least-one-dominant" floor, then merges each non-dominant expert into the most router-similar dominant expert in its layer. STUN+Wanda [32, 62] composes the behavioral-similarity Stage-1 of STUN with Stage-2 unstructured Wanda [62] sweep on survivor weights, following the released STUN implementation; the Stage-2 protocol is in App. B.4. HodgeCover (ours) follows the REAP convention with the uniform per-layer allocator of Eq. 32. The four ablations of HodgeCover. The four ablations probe two orthogonal levers of the HodgeCover construction: whether the triangle term contributes beyond the harmonic-edge term (Hodge No-Triangle), and whether the Hodge decomposition is load-bearing relative to topology-free or plain28 triplet alternatives at the same input data (Greedy-Barrier, Triplet-Penalty soft, Triplet-Hypergraph hard). Hodge No-Triangle reuses Algorithm 1s backbone unchanged and only modifies the coverage objective; the other three ablations replace survivor selection with greedy union-find merge sort and aggregate the resulting merge groups via the same frequency-weighted average as REAM and MC-SMoE (App. A.10). Hodge No-Triangle. Set λt = 0 in Eq. 10, dropping the triplet-triangle coverage term entirely and reducing Φ to saliency plus harmonic-edge coverage. The Step 1 harmonic-critical edge set is still extracted from the Hodge decomposition; only the triangle term is removed. This isolates the contribution of the triangle term holding the harmonic-edge term fixed. Greedy-Barrier (no topology). Sort all pairs {i, j} in ascending order of pairwise barrier bij and greedily union them via union-find loop that terminates when exactly components remain. No Hodge decomposition, no harmonic weighting, and no triplet input. This isolates the contribution of all topology against the cleanest topology-blind merge. Triplet-Penalty soft (no Hodge decomposition). Reuse Greedy-Barriers union-find loop with the edge cost bij replaced by bij (1 + αT pij), where pij is the layer-normalized mean of triplet barriers bijk over all (i, j, k) that contain edge {i, j} and αT 0 is the penalty strength. The same triplet table that HodgeCover consumes is consumed here, but the Hodge decomposition is skipped; this isolates the contribution of the harmonic kernel above plain sum-of-triplet-barriers softening. Triplet-Hypergraph hard (binary triangle veto). Reuse Greedy-Barriers union-find loop with hard triplet veto inspired by hypergraph-cut compression [72]: candidate union is accepted only if every triple of experts inside the resulting component (when of size 3) has bijk τT , where τT is the layers 50th-percentile triplet barrier; any union that would create component carrying high-barrier triple is rejected and the loop continues with the next-best edge. This is the hard-veto analogue of Triplet-Penalty soft and isolates the cost of binary triangle constraint vs. soft penalty. The four ablations cover 2 2 grid: triangle term on/off and Hodge decomposition on/off. HodgeCover is on/on, Hodge No-Triangle is off/on, Triplet-Penalty soft is on/off, and Greedy-Barrier is off/off. Triplet-Hypergraph hard is the hard-constraint variant of on/off. The matched-control hybrids REAP+Wanda and REAM+Wanda are not reported in their source papers but we run them here as fair-as-possible matched controls. The Stage 2 residual-sparsity protocol of App. B.4 is applied identically across all four hybrid methods. Table 4 reports DS-Avg and PPL for each (model, rate) cell. The pattern across the four hybrids is consistent. On the two Qwen scales at 33% rate the matchedcontrol hybrids cluster within 0.8 pp DS-Avg of each other; HodgeCover+W and REAP+W trade the lead per task but neither dominates. At 66% HodgeCover+W opens 0.30.8 pp DS-Avg gap over REAP+W and REAM+W and 513 pp gap over STUN+W on the two Qwen scales. On OLMoE1B-7B at 66% the four hybrids span an 8 pp DS-Avg range (HodgeCover+W 48.6%, STUN+W 45.1%, REAP+W 43.3%, REAM+W 40.8%); the small-model OLMoE regime is the noisiest of the three. HodgeCovers plan-time has one-time component (the simplicial complex, the pairwise and triplet barrier sweep, and the Hodge decomposition itself) that is computed once per backbone and cached across all subsequent compression rates, plus small per-rate component (the survivor-selection greedy loop and the redirect step, together within single-digit seconds). Reporting plan-time at the second compression rate run on backbone would therefore report the cached, single-digit number rather than the honest first-rate cost. We instead report plan-time at 33%, which on every backbone is the first rate run and the only rate at which the simplicial complex is built from scratch; the O((n2 + )Dd2) asymptotics that drive this cost are worked through in App. B.5. The same caching applies to the Stage 2 Wanda pruner: at the two Qwen scales the two HodgeCover rows in Table 7 agree within 10 (482.6 vs. 476.6 on Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B; 479.3 vs. 488.1 on Qwen 3.5-122B-A10B), because Stage 2 reuses the calibration tensor produced during Stage 1 with no additional forward pass; on the much smaller OLMoE-1B-7B the absolute plan-times are an order of magnitude smaller and the row-to-row spread (33.3 vs. 3.3 s) is dominated by one-time overheads in the from-scratch HodgeCover-only run rather than by Stage 2 itself. The plan-time gap to REAP+Wanda, by contrast, is real and structural: REAPs per-layer scoring is O(Dd2), dominated by the n2Dd2 pairwise sweep at the = 256-expert Qwen scales by factor of n2. Throughput, routing entropy, dead-expert. HodgeCover+Wanda matches REAP+Wandas tokensper-second to within 6% on every backbone (the largest gap is 370 tok/s, 6.0% relative, on OLMoE1B-7B); the routing entropy at 66% matches REAP+W and REAM+W to within 0.01 on the two Qwen scales. The dead-expert ratio at 66% favors REAM+Wanda by 13 pp absolute on the two Qwen scales (Qwen 3.5-35B-A3B: REAM+W 35.1% vs. HodgeCover+W 37.3%; Qwen 3.5-122BA10B: REAM+W 27.5% vs. HodgeCover+W 29.0%). We surface this as real disadvantage of HodgeCover+W in the cost picture. The dead-expert ratio is not, however, downstream-correlated metric in our data: the same REAM+W cells lose 0.30.8 pp DS-Avg to HodgeCover+W on the same backbones  (Table 4)  .
